title: Chapter Eleven: Maren's Pattern
content: I wake in my own bed, still wearing the clothes from last night.

The ceiling is a blank white expanse, and I stare at it the way you stare at a screen that's lost its signal — waiting for something to resolve. My mouth tastes like champagne and exhaustion. My right hand is curled against my chest, fingers hooked inward as though gripping something that isn't there.
I bring my hand to my face.

The scent hits me before the memory does — cedar and black pepper and something underneath, something warm and lived-in, like old books in a sunlit room. Rowan's cologne. It's soaked into my palm, into the creases of my fingers, into the thin skin between my knuckles where his hand pressed against mine on the dance floor.
I hold my hand against my nose and mouth and I breathe. Deep. The way you breathe when you're trying to memorize something you know you'll lose.
Then I hate myself for it.

I should be distancing. That was the whole point of leaving the gala — of walking away from him on that terrace with the city glittering behind us like a lie. I should be untangling, not cataloguing. Not lying here in yesterday's rumpled silk, filing away the pressure of his fingers between mine, the way his thumb traced the inside of my wrist, the warmth of his palm settling against the small of my back like it belonged there.
I press my hand harder against my face. The cologne is fading already. By noon it'll be gone, and I'll be left with nothing but the ordinary smell of my own skin.
Good. That's good.
I drop my hand. I sit up. I don't think about the way he looked at me when we danced — like I was a question he'd been asking his whole life and had finally, finally found the answer to.
I don't think about it.
Across town, in a white-walled studio on the ninth floor of a Garment District building that once housed a coat factory, Maren Voss is already working.

The studio is a surgical space — Eames furniture, matte surfaces, not a single object out of alignment. The morning light comes through floor-to-ceiling windows and bounces off the white epoxy floors with a glare that would punish lesser eyes. Maren's eyes are not lesser. They are instruments. They take in light and convert it to data.

She sits before a thirty-two-inch monitor, her posture perfect, her charcoal hair pulled into a knot so tight it seems to stretch the skin at her temples. On the screen: photographs from the Ashford Foundation gala. Hundreds of them, shot by the event photographer and already uploaded to the Foundation's press portal. Maren has access to the press portal. Maren has access to most things.

She scrolls with the deliberate pace of someone who knows exactly what she's looking for. Past the donors, past the board members, past the society column regulars with their practiced smiles and their borrowed jewels. She stops on image 147.
Orlando. Dancing with Rowan Kestral.

Maren had noticed it at the gala — the way the woman moved. Not the way she danced, though that was noteworthy enough. The way she *was*. As if her body had been drawn by someone with an obsessive commitment to proportion. As if the golden ratio had been applied to a human form and the result was this — this creature with the cheekbones of a Renaissance saint and the jaw of a Roman coin.

Maren zooms in. The resolution holds. Orlando's face fills the screen, turned three-quarter toward the camera, caught mid-laugh at something Rowan has said. The laugh is unguarded, and in the unguarded moment the architecture of her face is laid bare.

Maren opens a second window. She navigates to the Metropolitan Museum of Art's digital archive — a resource she has bookmarked, cross-referenced, and essentially memorized over the course of her career. Fashion designers who don't study art history are merely stylists. Maren Voss is not merely anything.
She types: *portrait, 1867, oil on canvas, European nobility*.
The search returns forty-seven results. She scrolls to the fourteenth.

*Portrait of a Young Nobleman*, attributed to the studio of Franz Xaver Winterhalter, 1867. Oil on canvas, 89 × 64 cm. Acquired by the Metropolitan Museum of Art, 1923, bequest of the Hargrove Collection.
Maren opens the image full-screen.

The young nobleman is perhaps twenty-five. Auburn hair swept back from a high forehead. Eyes that the painter rendered with such precision that the color — a shifting grey-green — seems to change depending on the light in the gallery. Cheekbones that could cut glass. A jaw squared and tapered with mathematical exactitude. A mouth that is neither smiling nor frowning but holding some private knowledge just behind the lips.
Maren places the two images side by side.
She leans forward. Her fingers hover over the keyboard, motionless.

The cheekbones. The eye shape — that slight upward tilt at the outer corners, almost feline. The set of the jaw, the arc from ear to chin. The distance between the pupils, the width of the nasal bridge, the precise geometry of the orbit.
Maren whispers: "That's not resemblance."
She studies the images for another full minute. The studio is silent except for the hum of the monitor and the distant groan of a freight elevator somewhere in the building's bones.
"That's replication."

Maren does not believe in the supernatural. She believes in genetics, in coincidence, in the human propensity for fraud, and in her own ability to tell the difference between all three. She believes in evidence. She believes in the archive.

She starts with the Winterhalter portrait. The sitter's name is listed in the Met's catalog as *Viktor, Count of Hohenberg (1842–1888)*. A minor Austrian nobleman who died at forty-six of tuberculosis, never married, no surviving children. No known portraits other than this one. Maren downloads the highest resolution image available and saves it to a new folder on her desktop.

Then she opens her research databases — the ones she pays for, the academic and auction platforms that give her access to exhibition records, provenance documents, photographic archives spanning two centuries. Maren's research skills are not a hobby. They are a competitive advantage. She once identified a misattributed Balenciaga from 1967 using nothing but a black-and-white photograph and knowledge of seam construction, and the resulting article established her as the most dangerous mind in fashion journalism. She does not guess. She verifies.
She begins with the face.

The bone structure she saw in Orlando — that specific, idiosyncratic architecture — is not common. The high zygomatic arches, the narrow orbital openings, the slight asymmetry in the left brow that suggests a break healed without setting. These are not generic features. They are as distinct as a fingerprint.
She queries: *portrait, photograph, anonymous, 1900–1950, comparable facial structure*.

The results are voluminous and useless. She refines: adds geographic parameters, date ranges, known exhibitions. Nothing. She tries portrait miniatures, daguerreotypes, tintypes. She's scrolling past an archive of wartime photographs from the Imperial War Museum when she stops.

The image is from 1943. A photograph of a woman in a utility coat, standing on a London street during the Blitz. Her face is turned three-quarter toward the camera — that same angle, as if the universe has a limited repertoire of poses for this particular set of bones. The cheekbones. The eyes. The jaw, squared and tapered. The slight left-brow asymmetry.
The bone structure is identical.
Not similar. Not reminiscent. *Identical*.

Maren clicks. The photograph enlarges. She prints it on the studio's archival printer — the paper stock is heavy and faintly textured, and when she picks the image from the output tray, the surface feels cool and smooth beneath her thumb, like handling a piece of evidence. Which is exactly what it is.
She pins it to the magnetic wall beside the Winterhalter portrait and the gala photograph. Three faces. Three different centuries. The same person.

No. Not the same person. That's impossible. The same *structure*. The same impossible recurrence of a genetic pattern that should dilute and disperse across generations but instead appears, intact and unaltered, in a woman dancing at a Manhattan gala in 2026.
Maren opens a new document. She types a single line:
**ORLANDO — IMPOSSIBLE**
Then she begins the file.
The file grows.

Maren works methodically, the way she designs a collection — gathering, curating, eliminating, refining. She finds a cartes-de-visite from 1881, Paris, featuring a young man at a society salon. The face is the same, rendered in the soft focus of early photographic processes, but the architecture is unmistakable. She adds it.

She finds a newspaper photograph from 1962, Milan Fashion Week — before it was called Fashion Week, when it was still a trade event for buyers. A figure in the background of a runway shot, watching from the wings. The resolution is poor, but the jawline is sharp enough to cut through the grain. She adds it.

Each image gets catalogued with date, source, provenance notes, and a confidence rating from one to ten. Maren's scale is her own, and she is rigorous. Most images rate a three or four — insufficient resolution, incomplete angle, possibility of pareidolia. But three images rate a nine: the Winterhalter portrait, the 1943 photograph, and the gala image.
Three nines, spanning 159 years.

Maren drinks her espresso. It has gone cold — she made it an hour ago and forgot it on the windowsill. The taste is bitter and flat, the milk skin congealed across the surface. She drinks it anyway. She's not thinking about coffee. She's thinking about authentication.

The Sotheby's incident. She'd heard about it third-hand — a cocktail party anecdote, one of those stories that gets passed around the art world like a ghost story around a campfire. Someone had corrected a misattribution on Lot 42, a nineteenth-century coat that had been catalogued as a minor Parisian workshop piece. The correction had been made on the spot, during the preview, by a woman who reportedly handled the garment and identified its provenance based on construction details invisible to the naked eye. The coat was subsequently re-attributed to the House of Worth and sold for nine times its estimate.

Maren picks up her phone. She calls a contact at Sotheby's — a junior cataloguer named David who owes her a favor after she identified a fake Hermès scarf in his personal collection and didn't publish the finding.
"David. The Lot 42 coat from last month's estate sale. The one that was misattributed. Who was the woman who corrected the authentication?"
A pause. The sound of David typing — keyboard clicks through the phone line, rapid and uncertain. "Hold on. It was during the preview event. Catering staff, I think — she was working the reception."
"A server corrected a Worth attribution?"
"That's what I heard. It was weird. She just — picked up the coat, turned it inside out, pointed to a seam, and said it was Worth. And she was right. Our specialists confirmed it."
"Name?"
"Let me check the roster." More typing. "The catering was contracted through the Ashford Foundation's preferred vendor. The name on the roster is just 'Orlando.' No last name."
"Orlando," Maren repeats. "Just Orlando."
"That's all I've got. Sorry."

Maren hangs up. She doesn't need David's apology. She needs the Ashford Foundation's records, and those are not a problem. She has contacts at the Ashford Foundation — the same way she has contacts everywhere that matters. A board member's wife wore Maren Voss to the Met Gala two years running. A development associate attended the same graduate program as Maren's studio manager. The world of institutional New York is a small world, and Maren maps it the way cartographers map coastlines — with patience, precision, and the understanding that every detail matters.

She drafts an email to the development associate. Casual tone, specific request: information about a catering employee named Orlando, any documentation on file, employment records, references. She frames it as a personal project — a profile she's considering for the magazine. This is both plausible and deniable. Maren has never written a profile she didn't intend to publish, and she intends to publish this one, eventually, the way one intends to publish an obituary: after the subject is no longer in a position to object.
She sends the email. She returns to the file.

The three faces stare at her from the magnetic wall — the nobleman, the woman in the utility coat, the dancer at the gala. Maren stands and walks to the wall. She studies them the way she studies a fabric swatch, looking for the flaw, the thread that doesn't belong, the evidence that the material is not what it claims to be.
She finds no flaw.
The faces are the same face.

Maren does not believe in reincarnation, in genetic memory, in the supernatural persistence of a soul across centuries. She believes in data. She believes in verifiable, publishable, headline-making data. And what she has here — if she can build the case, if she can make it airtight — is not a rival to be outdesigned.
It is a target to be exposed.

An anomaly. A living ghost. A woman who appears in the visual record of human history like a recurring decimal, the same face surfacing every few decades in a new context, a new country, a new century, untouched by time. If Maren can prove it — if she can build the forensic case that links these images beyond coincidence — the story would make international news. It would be discussed on morning shows and academic panels and late-night monologues. It would be debated and debunked and debated again. And at the center of every discussion, every article, every outraged think piece about identity and authenticity and the ethics of exposure, one name would appear:
Maren Voss.
Not as a designer. As a discoverer. As the woman who found the impossible and made it undeniably real.

Maren closes her laptop. The screen goes dark and she sees her own reflection — sharp, composed, exactly as she intended. She leans back in her white Eames chair and allows herself a single, precise moment of satisfaction.
Then she opens the laptop again. There's more work to do.

Outside the studio windows, the Garment District hums with its usual industry — delivery trucks and garment racks and the relentless machinery of commerce. Maren doesn't hear it. She hears only the click of her own keyboard, assembling the architecture of exposure, brick by meticulous brick.
She has started her hunt.
And Maren Voss is very, very good at hunting.
